name: El Cubillo - El Portillo Trail
name_original: Subida a los puertos del Cubillo y del Portillo — desde Tragacete
description: This difficult 26.0-mile touring cycling route through Parque Natural de la Serranía de Cuenca offers stunning views and a challenging climb
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Tragacete traverse a landscape characterized by the headwaters of major rivers like the Tagus and Cuervo, set within a mountainous environment. The region features significant elevation changes, with routes often climbing through forested areas and open mountain terrain. Gravel paths are common, providing varied surfaces for cycling. Best touring cycling routes around Tragacete The most popular touring cycling route is Empty Mountains: Guadalaviar Loop, a 31.6 miles (50.9 km) trail that takes 4 hours 31 minutes to complete. This route offers a challenging loop through the mountainous terrain surrounding the Guadalaviar river. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Empty Mountains Route, a difficult 38.7 miles (62.3 km) path. This trail explores expansive, remote mountain landscapes, featuring significant climbs and descents. Local touring cyclists also love the Puente de las Tres Provincias — circular gravel from the Nacimiento del Río Tajo, a 19.1 miles (30.7 km) trail leading through the area around the source of the Tagus River, often completed in about 2 hours 29 minutes. Touring cycling around Tragacete is defined by mountainous terrain, river valleys, and gravel paths. The network offers options for moderate to advanced touring cyclists. The routes in Tragacete are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from 7 reviews. More than 100 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Tragacete's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Tragacete?
A: There are over 15 touring cycling routes around Tragacete, offering a variety of experiences through the mountainous terrain and river valleys.

Q: What is the typical terrain like for touring cycling in Tragacete?
A: The terrain around Tragacete is predominantly mountainous, featuring significant elevation changes. You'll encounter a mix of forested areas, open mountain landscapes, and common gravel paths, providing a varied surface for touring cyclists.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Tragacete?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes. A notable one is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1094016335/puente-de-las-tres-provincias-circular-gravel-from-the-nacimiento-del-rio-tajo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de las Tres Provincias — circular gravel from the Nacimiento del Río Tajo&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 19.1-mile (30.7 km) trail.

Q: What kind of distances and elevations can I expect on these routes?
A: Routes vary significantly. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e956564587/empty-mountains-guadalaviar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Empty Mountains: Guadalaviar Loop&lt;/a&gt; covers 31.6 miles (50.9 km) with over 700 meters of elevation gain, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086082079/el-cubillo-el-portillo-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Cubillo - El Portillo Trail&lt;/a&gt; is 41.8 miles (67.3 km) with over 775 meters of ascent.

Q: Are there any family-friendly or beginner touring cycling routes?
A: While many routes in Tragacete are rated moderate to difficult due to the mountainous terrain and elevation changes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1094016335/puente-de-las-tres-provincias-circular-gravel-from-the-nacimiento-del-rio-tajo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de las Tres Provincias — circular gravel from the Nacimiento del Río Tajo&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate option that might be suitable for families or beginners looking for a challenge, offering a shorter distance and less extreme elevation compared to the more difficult trails.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4740317" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Cuervo River&lt;/a&gt;, the stunning &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4740327" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuervo River Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3925539" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the River Tagus&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also offer panoramic views, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/603722" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Serranía de Cuenca&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists?
A: Absolutely. The majority of routes in Tragacete are rated difficult. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086581860/empty-mountains-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Empty Mountains Route&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, is a difficult 38.7-mile (62.3 km) path exploring expansive, remote mountain landscapes with significant climbs and descents.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Tragacete?
A: Given the mountainous environment, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for touring cycling in Tragacete, with milder temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring colder weather and snow at higher elevations.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring bike trails in Tragacete?
A: Tragacete itself serves as a good starting point for many routes, and you can often find parking within the village. For routes starting from specific natural sites like the Nacimiento del Río Cuervo, dedicated parking areas are usually available nearby.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Tragacete?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from 7 reviews. Cyclists often praise the challenging mountainous terrain, the remote 'empty mountains' feel, and the stunning natural landmarks like the sources of the Tagus and Cuervo rivers.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by waterfalls?
A: Yes, several routes offer access to beautiful waterfalls. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086283639/nacimiento-del-rio-cuervo-circular-desde-tragacete" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Nacimiento del río Cuervo — circular desde Tragacete&lt;/a&gt; route will bring you close to the famous &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4740327" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuervo River Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5400567" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Molino Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find places to eat or drink along the touring cycling routes?
A: While some routes traverse remote areas, you'll find amenities in villages like Tragacete. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer routes, as cafes might not be directly on every trail. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3925562" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Refugio de la Alconera&lt;/a&gt; could be a point of interest for a break on some routes.
tips:
The Puerto de El Cubillo is a mountain pass located in the Montes Universales, in the region of Castilla-La Mancha. This port can be divided into two phases: a first one with a certain difficulty that goes from the beginning, at the intersection of the CM-2105 road, until kilometer 5.5, and then a practically flat phase that ends at the border of the provinces of Cuenca and Teruel.
It is an ideal place for lovers of cycling and hiking, offering spectacular views and an impressive natural environment.😎🚴🚵🚶‍♂️🌄
El Monolito forest shelter. Ideal place to spend the night indoors. There are no services other than a sleeping room and a common space to cook. Keep it clean and don't leave trash.
The port is ideal for outdoor activities such as hiking and cycling, and offers spectacular views of the Tagus Valley and the surrounding pine forests.😎🚵🚴🌄

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the El Cubillo - El Portillo touring cycling trail?
faqs.answer: The komoot tour suggests an average duration of approximately 2 hours and 55 minutes for touring cyclists. However, this can vary significantly based on your fitness level, the number of stops you make, and the specific route variations you choose.

faqs.question: What is the starting point for this touring cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route typically starts from Tragacete, a village located in the Serranía Alta de Cuenca region. This is a common access point for exploring the surrounding natural park.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for touring cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain around El Cubillo Pass and Puerto del Portillo features beautiful, winding roads nestled among trees for much of their length. You can expect mountainous sections with a satisfying challenge, offering a 'right dose of hardness' for touring cyclists.

faqs.question: Is the El Cubillo - El Portillo trail suitable for beginner touring cyclists?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating and the mountainous terrain of the Serranía Alta de Cuenca, this trail is generally not recommended for beginner touring cyclists. It requires good fitness and some experience with ascents and descents.

faqs.question: What can I see along the El Cubillo - El Portillo touring cycling route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll encounter significant landmarks such as the El Cubillo Pass and Puerto del Portillo. The route also passes by the Forest House Refuge. The region is known for its stunning natural beauty, offering opportunities to observe local wildlife like deer and roe deer.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the El Cubillo - El Portillo trail?
faqs.answer: The Serranía Alta de Cuenca region is best enjoyed for cycling during spring, summer, and early autumn. These seasons offer more favorable weather conditions, avoiding the colder temperatures and potential snow or ice that can affect mountainous passes in winter.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the El Cubillo - El Portillo touring cycling trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs are generally allowed on trails within the Parque Natural de la Serranía de Cuenca. However, it's always recommended to keep them on a leash, especially to protect local wildlife and ensure their safety on roads shared with vehicles.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are typically no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the El Cubillo - El Portillo trail or to access the Parque Natural de la Serranía de Cuenca. However, always respect local regulations and signage within the natural park.

faqs.question: Where can I park my vehicle when starting this cycling route?
faqs.answer: You can usually find parking in or around Tragacete, the village from which the ascent to El Cubillo and El Portillo typically begins. Look for designated parking areas or suitable roadside spots, ensuring you do not obstruct traffic or private access.

faqs.question: Does this touring cycling route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route covers significant sections of the official trail '[CIMA CU08] El Cubillo - El Portillo'. It also briefly intersects with parts of the 'Cañada Real Conquense o de los Chorros' and the 'Camino Natural del Tajo'.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see while cycling?
faqs.answer: The Serranía Alta de Cuenca is rich in biodiversity. Cyclists often have the chance to spot local wildlife such as deer and roe deer, especially during quieter times of the day. The natural park environment supports a variety of bird species as well.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or accommodation options near the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tragacete, the starting point for this route, offers various services including cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options suitable for cyclists. It's a good place to refuel or stay overnight before or after your ride.
